title: Alkanes — structure, naming and combustion
examTip: Alkanes are saturated (all single bonds, CnH2n+2). Complete combustion needs enough oxygen: fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. Incomplete combustion (limited O₂) produces CO and soot — both dangerous. Name from formula by counting carbons: 1=meth, 2=eth, 3=prop, 4=but.
introTips: Alkanes are the main components of natural gas and petroleum — the fuels that power most of the world right now.
The general formula CnH2n+2 lets you work out the molecular formula for any alkane if you know n.
Saturated means all C−C single bonds — no double bonds. This is why alkanes are relatively unreactive compared to alkenes.
Complete combustion always produces CO₂ and H₂O. Incomplete combustion produces CO (toxic) and/or carbon (soot) when oxygen is limited.
The first four alkanes — methane, ethane, propane, butane — must be memorised, including their formulas.
intro: Whenever you cook on a gas hob, fill up a car, or fly in a plane, you are using alkanes. These are the simplest organic molecules — chains of carbon atoms surrounded by hydrogen, held together by single bonds. They are the backbone of the fossil fuel industry and, increasingly, the focus of debates about climate change and air quality. Understanding their structure and how they burn is essential for chemistry — and for making sense of the energy challenges facing your generation.

keyPoints.heading: Structure and general formula of alkanes
keyPoints.content:
Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons — they contain only carbon and hydrogen atoms, joined by single C−C and C−H bonds only (no double bonds).

General formula: CnH2n+2 where n = number of carbon atoms.

First four alkanes:
• Methane: CH₄ (n=1) — main component of natural gas
• Ethane: C₂H₆ (n=2)
• Propane: C₃H₈ (n=3) — camping gas
• Butane: C₄H₁₀ (n=4) — lighter fuel

The carbon chain forms the backbone; each carbon forms exactly 4 bonds total. Alkanes with more carbons have higher boiling points and are less flammable as liquids — this matters in fractional distillation.
writeBullets: Alkanes: saturated hydrocarbons, only single C−C and C−H bonds
Methane CH₄, ethane C₂H₆, propane C₃H₈, butane C₄H₁₀
Higher carbons = higher boiling point, less flammable as liquid

keyTerms.def: Contains only single C−C bonds; no C=C double bonds
A compound containing hydrogen and carbon only
A saturated hydrocarbon with general formula CnH2n+2; all single bonds
A formula that applies to a whole homologous series; for alkanes: CnH2n+2

keyPoints.memTip: MEPB — Methane, Ethane, Propane, Butane. Or use the prefixes: meth=1, eth=2, prop=3, but=4. Then add 'ane' for alkanes.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students must be able to draw the structural (displayed) formula for the first four alkanes and derive any alkane formula from the general formula. Test this regularly.

keyPoints.heading: Complete combustion of alkanes
keyPoints.content:
Complete combustion occurs when there is a plentiful supply of oxygen. The products are always carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water (H₂O).

General word equation: alkane + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water

Balanced equations for the first four:
• CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O (methane)
• 2C₂H₆ + 7O₂ → 4CO₂ + 6H₂O (ethane)
• C₃H₈ + 5O₂ → 3CO₂ + 4H₂O (propane)
• 2C₄H₁₀ + 13O₂ → 8CO₂ + 10H₂O (butane)

Complete combustion releases more energy than incomplete combustion and is preferred for heating and engines.
writeBullets: Complete combustion: excess oxygen → CO₂ + H₂O always
Complete combustion releases more energy than incomplete

keyTerms.def: Burning with excess oxygen; always produces CO₂ and H₂O as the only products

keyPoints.memTip: Complete combustion: everything converted. C becomes CO₂, H becomes H₂O. No leftovers — you have enough oxygen to finish the job.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students need to be able to balance combustion equations. A good method: balance C first (→ CO₂), then H (→ H₂O), then count O atoms needed and put that number in front of O₂.

keyPoints.heading: Incomplete combustion — CO and soot
keyPoints.content:
Incomplete combustion occurs when there is a limited supply of oxygen. Instead of all the carbon becoming CO₂, some becomes carbon monoxide (CO) or unburnt carbon (soot/particulates).

Products of incomplete combustion: carbon monoxide (CO), carbon/soot (C), water (H₂O), and possibly some CO₂.

Why this is dangerous:
• Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colourless, odourless, toxic gas. It binds to haemoglobin in red blood cells more strongly than oxygen does, preventing oxygen transport — causing suffocation and death even in small concentrations.
• Soot (carbon particulates) causes respiratory problems and contributes to global dimming.

Examples: a yellow/orange flame on a gas cooker, diesel engines producing black smoke.
writeBullets: Incomplete combustion: limited oxygen → CO, soot (C), and H₂O
CO is colourless, odourless and highly toxic
CO binds to haemoglobin — prevents oxygen transport
Soot (carbon particulates) causes respiratory damage and global dimming
Orange/yellow flame = sign of incomplete combustion

keyTerms.def: Burning with insufficient oxygen; produces CO, soot (C), and water — CO is toxic
A toxic, colourless, odourless gas produced by incomplete combustion; binds to haemoglobin, preventing oxygen transport
Fine carbon particles produced by incomplete combustion; cause respiratory damage and global dimming

keyPoints.memTip: Incomplete = not enough O. C becomes CO instead of CO₂. CO = silent killer (colourless, odourless). Soot = black particles you can see in diesel exhaust.
keyPoints.tutor_note: In exam questions, students must clearly link CO to haemoglobin and oxygen transport — just saying 'it is toxic' is rarely enough for full marks.

keyPoints.heading: Naming alkanes from their formula
keyPoints.content:
To name an alkane, count the carbon atoms and use the correct prefix:
• 1 carbon → methane
• 2 carbons → ethane
• 3 carbons → propane
• 4 carbons → butane
• 5 carbons → pentane
• 6 carbons → hexane

All alkanes end in −ane. So C₅H₁₂ = pentane (5 carbons, 2×5+2 = 12 hydrogens ✓).

You can also go the other way: given the name (e.g. hexane, n=6), write the formula using CnH2n+2: C₆H₁₄.
writeBullets: Prefixes: meth(1), eth(2), prop(3), but(4), pent(5), hex(6)
Given formula: count C atoms → use prefix + ane
Given name: use n value in CnH2n+2 to find formula

keyTerms.def: A family of compounds with the same general formula and similar chemical properties; members differ by CH₂

keyPoints.memTip: My Enormous Purple Butterfly Penguin Hen: Meth, Eth, Prop, But, Pent, Hex — learn the first six prefixes in order.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Many students can name but cannot write the formula, or vice versa. Practise both directions — name → formula and formula → name.

tables.title: First six alkanes — names, formulas and uses

0.question: What is the molecular formula of propane?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Propane has 3 carbons. Using CnH2n+2: C₃H(2×3+2) = C₃H₈.
0.wrongFeedback: Use CnH2n+2 with n=3 for propane: C₃H(2×3+2) = C₃H₈. Not C₃H₆ — that would be an alkene (CnH2n).

1.question: Which products are formed by the COMPLETE combustion of methane?
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Complete combustion always produces CO₂ and H₂O: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O.
1.wrongFeedback: Complete combustion (excess oxygen) always produces CO₂ and H₂O. Carbon monoxide and soot are products of INCOMPLETE combustion (limited oxygen).

3.question: Why is carbon monoxide produced by incomplete combustion dangerous?
options: It binds to haemoglobin more strongly than oxygen, preventing oxygen transport in the blood
It is a greenhouse gas that causes climate change
It is a particulate that causes respiratory damage
It reacts with water to form acid rain
3.correctFeedback: Spot on! CO binds to haemoglobin in red blood cells far more strongly than oxygen does, preventing the blood from carrying oxygen to your tissues.
3.wrongFeedback: Carbon monoxide is dangerous because it binds to haemoglobin more strongly than oxygen, preventing your blood from transporting oxygen. It is colourless and odourless so you cannot detect it.

commonMistakes: Using the wrong general formula — alkanes are CnH2n+2, not CnH2n (which is alkenes). Check by substituting n=2: ethane = C₂H₆ ✓; CnH2n would give C₂H₄ which is ethene.
Writing incomplete combustion products as just CO₂ and H₂O — incomplete combustion produces CO and/or carbon (soot), not CO₂.
Saying CO is dangerous because it is 'poisonous' without explaining the mechanism — always link to haemoglobin binding and oxygen transport.
Forgetting to balance combustion equations — always check atom counts for C, H and O on both sides.
Confusing 'saturated' and 'unsaturated' — saturated means all single bonds (alkanes); unsaturated means at least one double bond (alkenes).
examTips: When writing combustion equations, balance in order: C (→ CO₂), then H (→ H₂O), then O. You can use fractions for O₂ then double the whole equation if whole numbers are required.
If asked to compare complete and incomplete combustion, always mention that incomplete combustion produces CO (toxic) and soot, and that complete combustion releases more energy.
For naming questions, always check your formula using CnH2n+2 after naming — if the hydrogen count does not match, you have made an error.
In describe questions about CO toxicity, you must mention haemoglobin — just 'it is poisonous' will not score the full mark.
revisionCardBullets: Alkanes: saturated hydrocarbons, only single bonds
Methane CH₄, ethane C₂H₆, propane C₃H₈, butane C₄H₁₀
Incomplete combustion (limited O₂): → CO + soot + H₂O
CO: colourless, odourless, toxic — binds to haemoglobin
Soot: carbon particulates — respiratory damage, global dimming
Prefixes: meth(1), eth(2), prop(3), but(4), pent(5), hex(6)
Blue flame = complete combustion; yellow/orange = incomplete
